The whole story of the trouble
between the Structural Building
Trades alliance and the Washing
ton Water Power company over the
new horse and car barns, has not
been told, according to George w.
Miller. business agent of the al
lance. So


AS the official representative Of
the alliance gives it, the trouble
originated in another broken prom
Use of the officers Of the Washing
ton Water Power company.


On Sept. 81, according to Bust
ness Agent Miller, several local
unions called on Superintendent
Harry Richards with a proposal
that all work on the barns be done
by union men, otherwise an effort
would be made to keep members
from working on the building. A
verbal agreement, it is claimed,
was made at this conference that
the company would remove all
non-union men employed on the
job. On Sept. Sit was found that
non-union men were still employed
on the barns and representatives
